publisher.publisher-name: Faculty of Veterinary Medicine, University of Tripoli

title-group.article-title: Mutation on Theileria species in cattle in Fallujah city, west region of Iraq

contrib-group.aff: 1Department of Parasitology, Faculty of Veterinary Medicine, Fallujah University, Fallujah, Iraq
2Department of Microbiology, Faculty of Medicine, Mustansiriyah University, Baghdad, Iraq
3Zoonotic Research Unit, Faculty of Veterinary Medicine, Baghdad University, Baghdad, Iraq
4Veterinary Hospital of Anbar, Veterinary office, Ministry of Agriculture, Baghdad, Iraq

author-notes.corresp: *Corresponding Author: Dunya A. A. Mohammed Salih. Department of Parasitology, Faculty of Veterinary Medicine, Fallujah University, Fallujah, Iraq. dr.dunya.abdal-malik@uofallujah.edu.iq

license.license-p: This is an Open Access article distributed under the terms of the Creative Commons Attribution Non-Commercial License (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc/4.0/) which permits unrestricted non-commercial use, distribution, and reproduction in any medium, provided the original work is properly cited.

sec.title: Background:
sec.p: Theileriosis infection is a tick-borne disease that leads to global animal morbidity, mortality, and economic losses.

sec.title: Aim:
sec.p: This study aimed to examine Theileriosis infection by traditional and molecular methods

sec.title: Methods:
sec.p: A total of 100 blood samples of local breed cows were examined by Giemsa smearing under a microscope, amplified-PCR in region 18SrRNA PCR at 600bp, and DNA sequencing analysis of the genetic relationships.

sec.title: Results:
sec.p: The clinical signs showed that an elevated fever (40.3°C–41.5°C), an asymmetrical extension of superficially prescapular lymph nodes, lacrimation, icteric mucus membranes eyes, ecchymotic hemorrhages on the sclera, dullness, lethargy, salivation, and ticks presence. Giemsa-stained blood smear and conventional PCR were revealed 24% and 73% for infection respectively. The phylogenetic tree was constructed with DNA sequencing analysis of accession number: OQ779946.1, OQ779947.1, OQ779949.1, OQ779952.1, OQ779953.1 and OQ779953.1, belonging to Theileria annulata; acc. nos.: OQ779950.1, OQ779951.1 belong to Theileria parva; acc. nos.: OQ779948.1, OQ779960.1 belongs to Theileria species; acc. no.: OQ779955.1, OQ779957.1, OQ779958.1 belong to T. sergenti; and OQ779956.1, OQ779959.1 belong to Theileria orientalis. Nucleotide substitutions of transversion and transitions occurred in 11 types containing gaps and missing are A>T; A>C; A>G; C>A; C>G; C>T; G>A; G>C; G>T; T>A; T>C for 254 positions in the final dataset.

sec.title: Conclusion:
sec.p: This study showed theileriosis infection is highly endemic and represents the first investigation into the use of molecular phylogeny to classify Theileria in Fallujah city, West Region of Iraq.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Theileria is a genus of tick-borne protozoa that causing theileriosis, leads to global animal morbidity, mortality, and economic losses (Abdullah et al., 2019). Eight different Theileria species cause bovine theileriosis including Theileria annulata, Theileria parva, Theileria orientalis, Theileria velifera, Theileria lowrenci, Theileria tarigtrgi, and Theileria sergenli that produce moderate or asymptomatic sickness in cattle (Khudor et al., 2022).

The most typical clinical signs initially are anorexia, elevated body temperature, enlargement in the superficial lymph node, pale mucus membranes, then iteration dehydration, saliva in the mouth, lacrimation, nose discharge, nervous signs, and breathing problems at the later stages (Faraj, and Assi, 2022). Theileriosis is often diagnosed by microscopy of blood and lymph smears with Giemsa-stained piroplasm and Koch’s bodies (Zaitoun et al., 2019). However, these methods have limitations in detecting carrier animals due to their low sensitivity or because of low parasitemia in animals, consequently, a negative result should not be regarded as confirmation of the absence of infection. Additionally, this method of diagnosis is unreliable since the morphology of Theileria species can vary during the infection course (Riaz et al., 2023). In contrast, modern immunological tests, mainly ELISA and indirect immune-fluorescent antibody tests, offer high sensitivity and specificity to show cross-reactivity with Abs-targeting other Theileria species (Bilgic et al., 2016). A PCR demonstrated very high sensitivity and specificity in identifying all species of Theileria (Akbari et al., 2012) are used in every epidemiology survey on theileriosis to avoid a cross reactivity that occurs when serological tests are used (Tsokana et al., 2023). So, this study detected an evolutionary position of Theileria species from Fallujah city, Iraq.

sec.title: General examination
sec.p: One hundred adult cows of local breeds of both sexes and various ages suspected of Theileriosis infection were chosen from various villages and at Hospital Veterinary Medicine College from the beginning of November 2022 to the end of March 2023 for clinical inspection by checking body temperature, palpating (prescapular and perfumery) lymph nodes, mucous membranes, respiration, congestion, lacrimation, emaciation, and tick infestation. Then, 3–5 ml of blood was drawn by sterile needle with a vacutainer EDTA- sterile tubes from the neck vein and immediately transported in the ice box to the parasitology laboratory.

sec.title: Laboratory examination
sec.p: Microscopic examination a fresh droplet of blood was put on a clear slide and then gently spread by a smooth border of another slide, leaving clear borders on both sides, to be air-dried, ethanol-fixed, 5% Giemsa’s-stained diluted in phosphate buffer [pH-7.2], for detection morphological characteristics of the parasite at 100x in oil immersion fields under microscopy (Al-Hosary and Nordengrahen, 2018).

sec.title: DNA isolation PCR reaction
sec.p: Genomic DNA was extracted from the 100 blood samples stored at −20ᵒC suspected of infection utilizing EasyPure® Blood Genomic DNA Kit (TransGen Company, China) according to the manufacturer’s instructions. DNA was tested by THERMO-Nano Drop (THERMO, USA) for its quantity and purity. 18S rRNA targeted primers )600 bp( designed for genus Theileria :F: 5’-TTTTTACATGGATAACCGTGC-3’; R:5’-AAAAGCCTGCTTTGAGCACT-3’ was ordered and applied by AccuPower® PCR-PreMix kit )Bioneer) as per instructions for the PCR technique (Chaudhri and Gupta, 2003; Allsopp et al., 1994). The 25 µl PCR final) 10 pmol/each direction( mixed volume was prepared by adding 1 ul /10 pmole of each primer to 1.5 µl of DNA into tubes kit, which contained ١٢.٥ µl of DNA polymerase [dNTPs, Tris-HCl, 10 mM of KCl, 1.5 mM of MgCl2], then finally completed by PCR water deionizer [9 µl]. The thermocycler reaction conditions included three steps: denaturation [94°C/3 minutes-1 cycle, and at 94°C/45 second], respectively, an annealing step [58°C/30 second-35 cycles], finally extension steps [72°C/1 minute] and 72°C/7 minutes-1 cycle. Electrophoresis was run for 1 hour / 80 volts and visualized PCR positively on the 1% agarose gel that was stained with a safety red stain using a UV transilluminator. Following electrophoresis, the proceeds of fifteen PCR products were purified using a gel purification and DNA clean-up kit (Promega, USA) following the manufacturer’s protocol.

sec.title: DNA sequencing and evolutionary analysis

p.italic: The purified positive PCRs (forward and reverse) were sent to Macrogen Company/Korea into an ice bag by DHL for the performed by ABI DNA sequencing system.

Phylogenetic analysis was done using the NCBI-Blast Alignment Method and phylogenetic analysis to determine the relationship between the local species isolates and others. Then, submitted to the NCBI-GenBank. The DNA sequencing analysis was conducted by BioEdit v. 7.0, and the multiple sequence alignment analysis of 18SrRNA gene was based on ClustalW alignment analysis.

sec.p: The tree is drawn to scale, and a sum of branch length (5.71975490) is shown. The evolutionary distances were computed using the Maximum Composite Likelihood method (Tamura et al., 2004) with the multitude of basis substitutions per site and clustered by the percentage of trees together (Felsenstein, 1985). Initial trees were obtained automatically by applying Neighbor-Join and BioNJ algorithms to a matrix of estimated pairwise distances. The proportion of sites where at least one unambiguous base is present in one sequence for each descendent clade is shown next to each internal node in the tree.

A comprehensive analysis of the nucleotide sequences of the 18SrRNA gene from the village districts of Fallujah city included evolutionary analyses of these sequences by software NCBI with their accession numbers assigned to the local Theileria Iraqi isolates in this study conducted in MEGA 6 (Kumar et al., 2018).

sec.title: Ethical approval
sec.p: The study was conducted under the Department of Parasitology, College of Veterinary Medicine: University of Fallujah. Animal Ethics Committee project number 24; data: 11.1.2024.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: Clinical manifestations of Theileria species were seen when particular animals were examined. These included an enlargement of the parotid and superficial prescapular lymph nodes, which were palpable as warm, painful, and swollen lumps. The animals showed evidence of a tick infestation, with weight loss, and a body temperature was [40.3°C–41.5°C], congestion of the mucous membrane, conjunctivitis, pale or jaundiced mucous membranes of the eye, and noticeable tearing (Fig. 1).

Microscopic examination of Giemsa-blood stained of the total 100 slides revealed an infection rate was 24% (24/100) of Theileria species in the cattle, which showed intra-erythrocytic ring, circular or rod-forms, comma-shaped, signal or pear-shaped forms with abnormalities in erythrocyte forms of piroplasm, others-shaped (Table 1, Fig. 2).
The conventional PCR results of the 18S rRNA gene [600 bp] revealed that 73% (73/100) of all tested blood samples were positive for Theileria species on agarose gel electrophoresis (Table 1; Fig. 3).

sec.title: DNA sequencing analysis
sec.p: The nucleotides identity percent of 18S ribosomal DNA genes in fifteen isolates of all local Theileria species from Fallujah city that local T. annulata isolates were compared between local Iraqi isolates and the GenBank-global strains, which ranged from 99% to 100% identity, had lengths [685 bp] with accession numbers (OQ779963.1; OQ779952.1) in comparison with three main clades, are shared 99% identically with the upper clade, including being very closely related to Italy (MT341858.1), Iran (MN960099.1), India (KT367878.1), Algeria (MH327773.1), Pakistan (MG599091.1), China (MK415058.1), Turkey (OR211419.1), and Egypt (MN625889.1) isolates; and identified 100% with the second and third clades that are closely related to France (AY150056.1), Spine (DQ287944.1), Sulaimani (MW940543.1) ,and Baghdad (MK182866.1) isolates as shown in (Fig. 4).

caption.title: Theileria species infection rate in cattle by blood smears and PCR.
Gel electrophoresis shows PCR results (1.5% agarose gel) with 18S rRNA gene (600 bp) of Theileria species, M: ladder (1,500-100 bp), Lanes positive: 1–12.

sec.p: Other local isolates as T. sergenti [666 bp], Theileria spp. [538 bp], and T. orientalis [562 bp] with 100% identical with different countries in the upper and middle clades compared to the T. parva [585 bp] isolates that were 99% identical related to the upper clade; the three isolates of T. sergenti are very much closer to Theileria spp. and grouped together with the Algeria (ON261355.1), two Chinese (DQ104610.1; JF719834.1) isolates and Thailand (AB000274.1), as well as USA (AF162431.1), and South Africa (MW647874.1) isolates. Also, local T. orientalis isolate shared with China (MH208640.1), Australia (AB520958.1) and Japan (XR696404.1) isolates; At last, two local isolates of T. parva (OQ779951.1; OQ779960.1) shared related to south African (HQ895971.1), Katete Zambia (MG952926.1), Kenya (MH929322.1), and Zimbabwe (KM211712) isolates, as shown in (Fig. 5).

caption.title: Analysis of tree phylogeny depended on 18S rRNA gene in local isolates of Theileria annulata (colour circles). MEGA v6.0 is used for evolutionary distances calculated.
Analysis of tree phylogeny depended on 18SrRNA gene in local isolates species of T.spp.; T.sergenti; T.orientalis; T.parva. MEGA v6.0 is used for evolutionary distances calculated.

sec.p: Mutation for 5 T. annulata isolates (OQ779946.1; OQ779949.1; OQ779952.1; OQ779953.1 and OQ779954.1) included 4 transversions at locations 162 and position (A/C); 493(T/A); and 306 (2A/T), respectively, and 5 transition on each location 6.5 and 6.6(A/G); 665(A/G); 466(A/G); 651(C/T), respectively; the other two isolate of Theileria species (OQ779948.1; OQ779960.1) included only 5 transversions at locations 197(G/C); 285(A/C); 412 (G/T): 197(G/C) and 213(G/C), respectively; T. parva has two isolates (OQ779950.1 and OQ779951.1) which have only 3 transversions at locations 206(A/T); 223(A/T) and 377(T/A), respectively; three isolates of T. sergenti (OQ779955.1; OQ779957.1; OQ779958.1) has two transversions at each location 463 (C/G); and one transition at location 279 (A/G); the last two isolates of T. orientalis (OQ779956.1; OQ779959.1) has two transversions at each location of 198 (A/C) and one transition at location 424 (G/A). An analysis includes 45 nucleotide sequences. The positions of codon were 1st, 2nd, and 3rd of noncoding. Nucleotide substitution of transversion and transitions occurred in eleven positions containing gaps and missing which are A>T, A>C, A>G, C>A, C>G, C>T, G>A, G>C, G>T, T>A, and T>C, for all total of 254 position of the final data set, as shown in (Table 2).

caption.title: Blasted-Theileria species with 18S ribosomal RNA gene and reference gene NCBI.

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: Our results revealed the cattle closely linked to the Theileria species via different numbers of tick-bites infestations that played a varying role in the severity of clinical symptoms such as anorexia and fever (40.3°C–41.5°C), as shown in (Fig. 1), and yellowish third eyelids were indicating superficial lymph node enlargement in theileriosis infection, which was supported by previous studies in Iraq (Minnat and Ibrahim, 2016; Verma and Singh, 2016).

The overall prevalence of Theileriosis in cattle from Fallujah villages was 24% (24/100) by microscopic blood Giemsa-stained smears and 73% by PCR technique. Many previous studies (Sajid et al., 2023) included this disease with prevalence in four directions in Iraq: in North Iraq, in Kurdistan region/Sulaimani province was 27.5% (22/80), and 31.25% (25/80), respectively (Ahmed et al., 2021); in Erbil 32.75% (95/290) (Aziz et al., 2022), in South Iraq, Al-Muthanna province was 13.23% (27/204) and 33% (33/100) (Tallaf and AL azawi, 2017); Al-Diwaniyah province 74% (37/50) and 62.2% (23/37) by microscopically and PCR test, respectively (Albayati et al., 2023), Bashra city (19.52%) by microscopic stained blood smears; In East of Iraq (AL-Mayah and Abdul-Karim, 2020), Diyala province was 100% (200/200) by using thin blood film (Minnat, and Ibrahim, 2016); middle Iraq, Baghdad of 39.33% (59/150) and 88% (22/25) using Gimsa blood smear and PCR respectively (Kawan, 2019). A comparison to other countries showed that based on 18S rRNA gene, the prevalence in Iran was (42/176) 23.9% (Noaman, 2013); and (174/844) 21% as reported by Parveen et al. (2021); Egypt, was (679/1068) 63.6% (Zaitoun et al., 2019); Oman, was 7.6% (Al-Fahdi et al., 2017) and Bangladesh, was 12.2% (47/385) by Hossain et al. (2023).

Conventional PCR with 18S rRNA region confirmed the Theileria species for detection and differentiation in a highly successful and sensitive assay of the carrier, subclinical, and infected animals, with low parasitemia in blood DNA (Aziz et al., 2022; Kamani et al., 2023; d’Oliveira et al., 1995). The microscopic method is a misdiagnosis for several species of Theileria staining confusion may arise on the morphological basis of piroplasm and schizont stages (Zeb et al., 2022) or attributed to carrier and chronic animals with low parasitemia (Al-Fahdi et al., 2017).

This variation of Theileria species prevalence in cattle among Iraqi cities is due to many different factors, as field management practices, efficient diagnostic tests used (Sajid et al., 2023), cattle-breed numbers examined, tick control programs distribution or resistance, animals susceptibility to infection inside the field, immunization, prophylactic strategy, and treatment (Aziz et al., 2022; Coles, 2001).

Other factors include environmental and climate changes [temperatures and humidity], which have an impact on the density of ticks in animals, leading to outbreaks of diseases, especially of the Ixodidae family to facilitate tick migration and the subsequent development of parasites in new areas (Abdullah et al., 2019; Gupta et al., 2020).

Since Fallujah shows an endemic area with the disease, animal migration among farms or countries is vital for the spreading and resistance of tick-borne disease against the use of the drug, which agrees with the reference sequence of Theileria isolates in Tunisia (Mhadhbi et al., 2015) showed multiple point substitutions in cytb-gene were help for the genera resistance of Theileria against buparvaquone and phylogeny relationship suggests a large levels of gene resistance against benzimidazole drug in migratory animals (Chatanga et al., 2019), or due levels of amino acid residues with different lows of drug-bind sites leading to failure in the actions of drug (Mhadhbi et al. 2015; Lahig et al., 2017; Ponnudurai et al., 2022).

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: Our study data provide an analysis of 18SrRNA is involved in the resistance in Theileria isolates of cattle under natural field conditions. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first study that confirmed a morphological finding with DNA sequences of Theileria species in local breeds cows in Fallujah city, West of Iraq. Results showed evolution in the genetics of local strains compared with GeneBank data, and drove out the resistance mitigation strategies on molecular surveys on a large number of animals from the whole country, so we need to perform correct prevalence and geographical distribution for all species of Theilera to help in the design of effective drugs and control measures.

ack.title: Acknowledgment
ack.p: The authors thank all academic professors for helping in the College of Veterinary Medicine, University of Fallujah.

sec.title: Funding
sec.p: The authors received no funding from industry, funding agency, or elsewhere to conduct this study.

p.italic: Study design: Dunya A.A. Mohammed Salih and Lina A. Jebur. Acquisition of blood specimens: Dunya A.A. Mohammed Salih, Noor A. Altaii, and Lina A. Jebur. Procedure of molecular process and analysis: Dunya A.A. Mohammed Salih and Noor A. Altaii. Analysis data: Lina A. Jebur and Wurood S. Ibadi. Drafting of the manuscript: Dunya A.A. Mohammed Salih and Lina A. Jebur. Writing-review and editing: All authors read and approved the final manuscript.

sec.title: Conflict of interest
sec.p: The authors declared no competing interests.

sec.title: Data availability
sec.p: All data are provided in the manuscript, further enquiries can be directed to the corresponding author.
